g.data-narration: Welcome to Module 3: Architectural Design of Compute and Storage Clouds. Let's explore how clouds are built.
g.text: Module 3: Architectural Design of Compute and Storage Clouds

g.data-narration: Cloud architecture is designed as loosely coupled services that can be dynamically provisioned and managed.
g.text: • Collection of loosely coupled services that can be
• Foundation for scalability, reliability, and efficiency

g.data-narration: Compute and storage are separated architecturally to allow independent scaling based on specific workload needs.
The four-layer cloud architecture: Hardware → Infrastructure (IaaS) → Platform (PaaS) → Application (SaaS).
The Hardware Layer is the physical foundation: servers, routers, switches, power, and cooling systems in secure datacenters.
The Infrastructure Layer uses hypervisors to create and manage Virtual Machines, delivering IaaS like AWS EC2.
Platform Layer provides middleware, databases, and APIs for development. Application Layer offers end-user apps via browsers.
g.text: AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Google App Engine

g.data-narration: The Virtual Machine is the fundamental compute unit. A hypervisor on physical hosts creates and runs guest VMs.
g.text: • VM = fundamental compute unit in cloud

g.data-narration: Resource pooling aggregates CPU and RAM from multiple hosts into a large pool. The orchestrator draws resources when VMs are requested.
Elasticity enables automatic scaling. Scale-out adds VMs during high traffic; scale-in removes them when demand decreases.
Storage clouds offer three types: Object for unstructured data, Block for low-latency apps like databases, File for shared file systems.
g.text: Storage Cloud: Three Types of Storage
Images/Videos → Object | DBs → Block | Shared Content → File

g.data-narration: Object storage uses flat namespace with unique IDs. Block storage attaches raw volumes to VMs formatted with filesystems.
Storage clouds achieve durability by replicating data across multiple geographic zones, protecting against hardware failure and disasters.
Compare compute vs storage: compute focuses on processing with elasticity; storage prioritizes durability through replication.
g.text: Summary: Compute Cloud vs Storage Cloud

g.data-narration: The decoupled architecture separating compute and storage enables core cloud characteristics: on-demand service, resource pooling, and rapid elasticity.
This architectural foundation enables the five essential cloud characteristics: on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, and measured service.
g.text: Core Cloud Characteristics Enabled by This Architecture
On-demand Self-service • Broad Network Access • Resource Pooling • Rapid Elasticity • Measured Service
